title: Power of 2
description: Check if number is power of 2

approaches.name: Brute Force O(log n)
approaches.summary: Iteratively divide the number by 2 until it becomes 1 or a non-integer, checking if it remains an integer throughout. Use this for clarity and educational purposes when introducing the problem.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Power of Two Check
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of iterative division approach to determine if a number is a power of two
ladders.explanation: This is the complete solution using the brute force O(log n) approach. The intuition is that a power of two has exactly one '1' bit in its binary representation, but we're using the mathematical property: if a number n is a power of two, repeatedly dividing by 2 will eventually reach 1 without encountering any non-integer divisions. We start with the given number and keep dividing by 2 until we either reach 1 (success) or encounter a non-integer division (failure). The algorithm handles edge cases: numbers ≤ 0 are immediately false since powers of two are positive. The step-by-step algorithm: 1) If n ≤ 0, return false. 2) While n > 1: a) If n % 2 ≠ 0, return false. b) n = n / 2. 3) Return true. Time complexity is O(log n) because we divide by 2 each iteration, and space complexity is O(1) using only constant extra variables. This connects to L1 by implementing the complete check using the division subroutine. Common mistakes include: forgetting to handle negative numbers and zero (which aren't powers of two), integer overflow (not an issue here as we're dividing), and infinite loops with n=1 (handled by while n>1). Real code pattern: def isPowerOfTwo(n): if n <= 0: return False; while n > 1: if n % 2 != 0: return False; n = n // 2; return True.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Iterative Division by Two
ladders.desc: Implement the division loop that reduces a number by half while checking divisibility
ladders.explanation: This is the most important helper function: repeatedly dividing a positive integer by 2 until it becomes 1, checking at each step if the division leaves no remainder. The intuition is that for a number to be a power of two, it must be divisible by 2 at every step of reduction to 1. We assume the input is positive (edge cases handled at higher level). The step-by-step algorithm: 1) While n > 1: a) If n % 2 ≠ 0, return false. b) n = n / 2. 2) Return true. Time complexity is O(log n) as we perform up to log₂(n) divisions, and space is O(1). This connects to L0 by being the core loop of the full solution, and to L2 as it implements the divisibility check logic. Common mistakes include: not using integer division (// in Python) leading to floats, off-by-one errors with n=1 (while n>1 handles this), and not checking remainder before dividing. Real code pattern: def divideByTwoUntilOne(n): while n > 1: if n % 2 != 0: return False; n = n // 2; return True.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Divisibility Check by Two
ladders.desc: Determine if a number is divisible by 2 without remainder
ladders.explanation: This is the central insight: checking if a number is divisible by 2 by examining its remainder when divided by 2. The intuition is that a number divisible by 2 has remainder 0, which is the fundamental property needed at each step of the power-of-two verification. For any integer n, we compute n % 2: if result is 0, n is divisible by 2; if 1, it's not. The algorithm: 1) Compute remainder = n % 2. 2) If remainder == 0, return true; else false. Time and space are O(1). This connects to L1 as the condition checked in each iteration of the division loop, and to L3 as it uses the modulo operation. Common mistakes include: confusing % with /, not handling negative numbers correctly (but for power of two we only need positives), and using floating-point division. Real code pattern: def isDivisibleByTwo(n): return n % 2 == 0.

examples.trace: n=8 → 8%2=0 → 0==0 → Return True
n=7 → 7%2=1 → 1==0? No → Return False

ladders.title: Building Block — Modulo Operation
ladders.desc: Compute the remainder when one integer is divided by another
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is the modulo operation (%), which gives the remainder of division. The intuition is that when we divide a by b, we get quotient and remainder; modulo returns the remainder. For example, 7 % 3 = 1 because 7 divided by 3 is 2 with remainder 1. The algorithm: given integers a and b (b ≠ 0), compute a % b = a - (a // b) * b. Time and space are O(1). This connects to L2 as it's used to check divisibility by 2, and to L4 as it involves arithmetic operations. Common mistakes include: using modulo with b=0 (division by zero error), misunderstanding negative operands (in Python, remainder has same sign as divisor), and confusing with division /. Real code pattern: def modulo(a, b): return a % b.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Integer Division
ladders.desc: Divide two integers and return the quotient as an integer
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation is integer division (// in Python), which divides two integers and truncates to an integer result. The intuition is that it performs division but keeps only the whole number part, discarding any fractional remainder. For example, 7 // 3 = 2 because 3 fits into 7 twice. The algorithm: given integers a and b (b ≠ 0), compute a // b = floor(a / b). Time and space are O(1). This connects to L3 as it's used in the modulo calculation and in the main division step, and to L5 as it builds on arithmetic. Common mistakes include: division by zero, confusing with float division (/), and not understanding truncation toward negative infinity in Python. Real code pattern: def integerDivide(a, b): return a // b.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — While Loop with Condition
ladders.desc: Execute a block of code repeatedly while a condition is true
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: a while loop that repeats actions as long as a condition holds. The intuition is to automate repetitive tasks without writing duplicate code. The structure: while condition: execute statements. The condition is checked before each iteration; if true, the loop body runs; if false, the loop ends. Time depends on iterations; space is O(1) if no extra data structures. This connects to L4 as it controls the division iterations. Common mistakes include: infinite loops (if condition never becomes false), off-by-one errors, and not updating variables in the loop. Real code pattern: def whileExample(n): while n > 0: print(n); n = n - 1.

examples.trace: n=3 → n>0? Yes → print(3) → n=2 → n>0? Yes → print(2) → n=1 → n>0? Yes → print(1) → n=0 → n>0? No → Stop
n=1 → n>0? Yes → print(1) → n=0 → n>0? No → Stop

approaches.name: Bitwise AND O(1)
approaches.summary: Use the property that a power of two has exactly one '1' bit in binary, so n & (n-1) equals 0 for n > 0. This is the most efficient and common solution in practice.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Check if Number is Power of Two Using Bitwise AND
ladders.desc: Complete solution using n & (n-1) == 0 property for n > 0
ladders.explanation: This is the complete solution to determine if a given integer n is a power of two. The core insight leverages binary representation: powers of two have exactly one '1' bit. For example, 1 (binary 1), 2 (10), 4 (100), 8 (1000). The key property is that for n > 0, if n is a power of two, then n & (n-1) equals 0. Why? Consider n = 8 (1000). n-1 = 7 (0111). The bitwise AND of 1000 and 0111 is 0000. For non-powers like n = 6 (0110), n-1 = 5 (0101), AND gives 0100 ≠ 0. The algorithm: First, handle edge case n ≤ 0 (negative numbers and zero are not powers of two). Then, compute n & (n-1) and check if result is 0. Time complexity is O(1) as bitwise operations are constant time. Space complexity is O(1) using only a few variables. This connects to L1 by implementing the complete function with input validation. Common mistakes: Forgetting to check n > 0 (since n=0 gives 0 & (-1) which might be 0 but 0 is not a power of two), or misunderstanding bitwise AND operation. Real code pattern: def isPowerOfTwo(n): if n <= 0: return False; return (n & (n-1)) == 0.

examples.trace: Step 1: n = 8. Check n > 0: 8 > 0, proceed. Step 2: Compute n-1 = 7. Step 3: Binary: n = 8 (1000), n-1 = 7 (0111). Step 4: Compute n & (n-1): 1000 AND 0111 = 0000 (0). Step 5: Check if result == 0: 0 == 0, so return True.

Step 1: n = 6. Check n > 0: 6 > 0, proceed. Step 2: Compute n-1 = 5. Step 3: Binary: n = 6 (0110), n-1 = 5 (0101). Step 4: Compute n & (n-1): 0110 AND 0101 = 0100 (4). Step 5: Check if result == 0: 4 != 0, so return False.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Compute n & (n-1) and Check Zero
ladders.desc: Implement the bitwise AND operation between n and n-1 and verify if result is zero
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the full solution. Given an integer n (assumed n > 0), it computes n & (n-1) and returns whether the result equals 0. The intuition: In binary, subtracting 1 from a number flips all bits from the least significant '1' bit to the rightmost bit. For powers of two, which have a single '1' bit at some position, n-1 will have '1's in all lower bits, making n and n-1 have no overlapping '1' bits, so their AND is 0. For example, n=4 (100), n-1=3 (011), AND=0. For non-powers, n has multiple '1' bits, so n-1 will not flip all bits in a way that eliminates overlap, leaving a non-zero AND. Algorithm: Input n. Compute temp = n-1. Compute result = n & temp. Return (result == 0). Time: O(1) for arithmetic and bitwise ops. Space: O(1). This feeds into L0 by providing the core check after validating n > 0. Common mistakes: Not ensuring n is positive (handled in L0), or using logical AND instead of bitwise AND. Real code pattern: def checkPowerBitwise(n): return (n & (n-1)) == 0.

examples.trace: Step 1: n = 16. Step 2: Compute n-1 = 15. Step 3: Binary: n = 16 (10000), n-1 = 15 (01111). Step 4: Compute n & (n-1): 10000 AND 01111 = 00000 (0). Step 5: Check 0 == 0, return True.
Step 1: n = 12. Step 2: Compute n-1 = 11. Step 3: Binary: n = 12 (01100), n-1 = 11 (01011). Step 4: Compute n & (n-1): 01100 AND 01011 = 01000 (8). Step 5: Check 8 == 0? No, return False.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Property of Powers of Two in Binary
ladders.desc: Understand that powers of two have exactly one '1' bit in binary representation
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the fundamental insight: any integer that is a power of two (like 1,2,4,8,...) has a binary representation with exactly one '1' bit and all other bits '0'. For example, 2^0=1 (1), 2^1=2 (10), 2^2=4 (100), 2^3=8 (1000). This is because powers of two are numbers like 2^k, which in binary is 1 followed by k zeros. The intuition comes from how binary system works: each bit represents a power of two (1,2,4,8,...), so a single '1' bit means the number is exactly one of those powers. This property is key because it allows using bitwise operations to detect such numbers efficiently. Algorithm to verify: Given n, convert to binary (conceptually), count number of '1' bits—if count == 1, it's a power of two. But we use a smarter method in L1. Time to check by counting bits could be O(log n), but we aim for O(1). This connects to L1 by explaining why n & (n-1) works: for a number with one '1' bit, subtracting 1 flips that bit to 0 and all lower bits to 1, making AND zero. Common mistakes: Confusing with numbers that have one '1' but are not powers of two (impossible since binary position determines value), or missing that 0 has no '1' bits but is not a power of two. Real code pattern: Conceptually, use bin(n).count('1') == 1 for intuition.

examples.trace: Step 1: n = 8. Step 2: Convert to binary: 8 in decimal is 1000 in binary. Step 3: Count '1' bits: '1000' has one '1' bit. Step 4: Since count == 1, conclude 8 is a power of two.
Step 1: n = 10. Step 2: Convert to binary: 10 in decimal is 1010 in binary. Step 3: Count '1' bits: '1010' has two '1' bits. Step 4: Since count != 1, conclude 10 is not a power of two.

ladders.title: Building Block — Bitwise AND Operation
ladders.desc: Perform bitwise AND between two integers to find common '1' bits
ladders.explanation: The bitwise AND operation is a fundamental building block for the solution. Given two integers a and b, it computes a new integer where each bit is 1 only if the corresponding bits in a and b are both 1; otherwise, it's 0. Symbolized as a & b. Intuition: It acts as a mask to extract common set bits. For example, a=6 (0110), b=5 (0101), a & b = 4 (0100) because only the bit at position 2^2 is 1 in both. In binary: 0110 AND 0101 = 0100. This is crucial because in L2, we need to check if n and n-1 have no common '1' bits. Algorithm: Represent a and b in binary (typically done internally by computer). For each bit position i (from least to most significant), compute result_bit = a_bit AND b_bit (where bits are 0 or 1). Combine bits to form result. Time: O(1) as it operates on fixed-size integers (e.g., 32 or 64 bits). Space: O(1). This feeds into L2 by enabling the check for single '1' bit property via n & (n-1). Common mistakes: Confusing with logical AND (which returns True/False), or not understanding binary representation. Real code pattern: result = a & b.

examples.trace: Step 1: a = 5 (binary 0101), b = 3 (binary 0011). Step 2: Perform bitwise AND for each bit: Bit 0: 1 AND 1 = 1. Bit 1: 0 AND 1 = 0. Bit 2: 1 AND 0 = 0. Bit 3: 0 AND 0 = 0. Step 3: Result binary: 0001. Step 4: Convert to decimal: 1.

Step 1: a = 12 (binary 1100), b = 10 (binary 1010). Step 2: Bitwise AND: Bit 0: 0 AND 0 = 0. Bit 1: 0 AND 1 = 0. Bit 2: 1 AND 0 = 0. Bit 3: 1 AND 1 = 1. Step 3: Result binary: 1000. Step 4: Decimal: 8.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Binary Representation of Integers
ladders.desc: Convert an integer to its binary form and understand bit positions
ladders.explanation: This level covers how integers are represented in binary (base-2) system, which is essential for bitwise operations. In binary, each digit (bit) is either 0 or 1, and positions represent powers of two: rightmost bit is 2^0 (1), next is 2^1 (2), then 2^2 (4), etc. For example, decimal 5 is binary 101: 1*4 + 0*2 + 1*1 = 5. Intuition: Binary is fundamental to computer arithmetic because processors operate on bits. To convert decimal to binary: repeatedly divide by 2 and record remainders (0 or 1) from last to first. For n=6: 6/2=3 rem 0, 3/2=1 rem 1, 1/2=0 rem 1 → binary 110. This operation is basic because in L3, we need binary forms to perform AND. Time to convert is O(log n) but for fixed-size ints, it's constant. Space: O(log n) for representation. This feeds into L3 by providing the input bits for AND. Common mistakes: Off-by-one in bit positions, or confusing with other bases. Real code pattern: Use bin(n) in Python to get string representation, e.g., bin(5) returns '0b101'.

examples.trace: Step 1: n = 8. Step 2: Divide by 2: 8/2=4 rem 0. Step 3: 4/2=2 rem 0. Step 4: 2/2=1 rem 0. Step 5: 1/2=0 rem 1. Step 6: Read remainders backwards: 1,0,0,0. Step 7: Binary: 1000.
Step 1: n = 11. Step 2: 11/2=5 rem 1. Step 3: 5/2=2 rem 1. Step 4: 2/2=1 rem 0. Step 5: 1/2=0 rem 1. Step 6: Remainders backwards: 1,0,1,1. Step 7: Binary: 1011.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Integers and Basic Arithmetic
ladders.desc: Understand integers, positive/negative numbers, and simple subtraction
ladders.explanation: This is the foundational concept: integers are whole numbers including positives, negatives, and zero. For this problem, we focus on non-negative integers initially. Basic arithmetic operations like subtraction are needed: given n, compute n-1. Intuition: Subtraction reduces a number by 1, which in binary will change the bits, crucial for the bitwise trick. For example, if n=4, then n-1=3. This is simple math but underpins the algorithm. Also, understanding that numbers like 0 or negatives are not powers of two (since 2^k > 0 for integer k). Algorithm: Input n. Output n-1 by subtracting 1. Time: O(1). Space: O(1). This feeds into L4 because binary representation depends on the integer value. Common mistakes: Not handling zero or negatives correctly (covered in higher levels), or overflow in large numbers (not an issue in Python). Real code pattern: m = n - 1.

examples.trace: Step 1: n = 5. Step 2: Subtract 1: 5 - 1 = 4. Step 3: Output 4.
Step 1: n = 1. Step 2: Subtract 1: 1 - 1 = 0. Step 3: Output 0.

approaches.name: Logarithm O(1)
approaches.summary: Compute the logarithm base 2 of the number and check if the result is an integer using floating-point arithmetic. Suitable when bitwise operations are not preferred, but beware of precision issues.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Check Power of 2 Using Logarithm
ladders.desc: Complete solution using logarithm base 2 with floating-point arithmetic to determine if a number is a power of two.
ladders.explanation: This approach leverages the mathematical property that if a number n is a power of two, then log₂(n) must be an integer. The intuition is that powers of two follow the sequence 1, 2, 4, 8, 16... where each is 2 raised to an integer exponent. By computing log₂(n) and checking if the result is an integer (within floating-point precision), we can verify this property. The algorithm first handles edge cases: n must be positive (since powers of two are positive), and n=0 is not a power of two. Then, it computes log₂(n) using the natural logarithm: log₂(n) = ln(n)/ln(2). Next, it checks if this value is an integer by comparing it to its floor value (using math.floor or equivalent). Due to floating-point precision issues, we add a small epsilon tolerance (e.g., 1e-10) when comparing. The time complexity is O(1) as it involves constant-time mathematical operations, and space complexity is O(1) using only a few variables. This connects to L1 by relying on the integer check of the logarithm result. Common mistakes include not handling negative numbers or zero, ignoring precision errors leading to false negatives/positives, and integer overflow in languages with fixed precision. Real code pattern: import math; def isPowerOfTwo(n): if n <= 0: return False; log_val = math.log(n) / math.log(2); return abs(log_val - round(log_val)) < 1e-10.

examples.trace: Step 1: Input n = 16. Step 2: Check if n > 0: 16 > 0, so proceed. Step 3: Compute log₂(16) = ln(16)/ln(2). ln(16) ≈ 2.772588722, ln(2) ≈ 0.693147181, so log₂(16) ≈ 4.0. Step 4: Check if log_val is integer: abs(4.0 - round(4.0)) = 0 < 1e-10, so True. Step 5: Return True.

Step 1: Input n = 18. Step 2: Check if n > 0: 18 > 0, so proceed. Step 3: Compute log₂(18) = ln(18)/ln(2). ln(18) ≈ 2.890371758, ln(2) ≈ 0.693147181, so log₂(18) ≈ 4.169925001. Step 4: Check if log_val is integer: abs(4.169925001 - round(4.169925001)) = abs(4.169925001 - 4) = 0.169925001 > 1e-10, so False. Step 5: Return False.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Check if Logarithm Result is Integer
ladders.desc: Determine if a floating-point number represents an integer value within precision tolerance.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is crucial because the logarithm approach computes a floating-point value, and we need to verify if it's an integer to confirm the power-of-two property. The intuition is that due to floating-point arithmetic limitations (e.g., rounding errors), direct equality checks (like log_val == int(log_val)) may fail. Instead, we check if the difference between the value and its nearest integer is within a small epsilon (e.g., 1e-10). The algorithm takes a float x as input, computes its rounded value (using round(x) or similar), and checks if abs(x - rounded_x) < epsilon. If true, x is considered an integer. Time complexity is O(1) for basic arithmetic, and space is O(1). This feeds into L0 by providing the integer verification step after computing log₂(n). Common mistakes include using a fixed epsilon that's too large or small, not handling negative x correctly, and integer conversion errors. Real code pattern: def is_integer(x, epsilon=1e-10): return abs(x - round(x)) < epsilon.

examples.trace: Step 1: Input x = 4.0. Step 2: Compute round(4.0) = 4. Step 3: Compute abs(4.0 - 4) = 0. Step 4: Check if 0 < 1e-10: True. Step 5: Return True.
Step 1: Input x = 4.169925001. Step 2: Compute round(4.169925001) = 4. Step 3: Compute abs(4.169925001 - 4) = 0.169925001. Step 4: Check if 0.169925001 < 1e-10: False. Step 5: Return False.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Compute Logarithm Base 2 of a Number
ladders.desc: Calculate log₂(n) using the change-of-base formula with natural logarithms.
ladders.explanation: The core logic involves computing the logarithm base 2 of a given number n, which is essential because powers of two have integer log₂ values. The intuition is that log₂(n) answers the question: 'To what power must 2 be raised to get n?' We use the change-of-base formula: log₂(n) = ln(n)/ln(2), where ln is the natural logarithm (base e). This works because logarithms can be converted between bases using division. The algorithm takes a positive number n, computes ln(n) and ln(2) using a math library (e.g., math.log in Python), and returns their quotient. Time and space complexity are O(1). This connects to L1 by providing the float value to check for integer-ness. Common mistakes include not ensuring n is positive (ln of non-positive is undefined), precision loss in division, and misunderstanding logarithm properties. Real code pattern: import math; def log_base_2(n): return math.log(n) / math.log(2).

examples.trace: Step 1: Input n = 8. Step 2: Compute ln(8) ≈ 2.079441542. Step 3: Compute ln(2) ≈ 0.693147181. Step 4: Compute quotient: 2.079441542 / 0.693147181 ≈ 3.0. Step 5: Return 3.0.
Step 1: Input n = 10. Step 2: Compute ln(10) ≈ 2.302585093. Step 3: Compute ln(2) ≈ 0.693147181. Step 4: Compute quotient: 2.302585093 / 0.693147181 ≈ 3.321928095. Step 5: Return 3.321928095.

ladders.title: Building Block — Change-of-Base Formula for Logarithms
ladders.desc: Convert a logarithm from one base to another using division of logarithms.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is needed because we often compute logarithms in a specific base (like natural log) but need them in another base (like base 2). The intuition is that logarithms are exponents, and changing the base is like scaling the exponent. The change-of-base formula states: logₐ(b) = log_c(b) / log_c(a), for any positive bases a and c (not equal to 1). This works because if a^x = b, then taking log_c of both sides gives x * log_c(a) = log_c(b), so x = log_c(b)/log_c(a). The algorithm takes values a, b, and c (with a,b>0, a≠1, c>0, c≠1), computes log_c(b) and log_c(a) using a math function, and returns their quotient. Time and space are O(1). This feeds into L2 by allowing computation of log₂(n) using natural logarithms. Common mistakes include using invalid bases (like 0 or 1), not handling negative inputs, and precision errors. Real code pattern: import math; def change_of_base(a, b, base_c=math.e): return math.log(b, base_c) / math.log(a, base_c).

examples.trace: Step 1: Input a=2, b=8, c=e (natural log). Step 2: Compute ln(8) ≈ 2.079441542. Step 3: Compute ln(2) ≈ 0.693147181. Step 4: Compute quotient: 2.079441542 / 0.693147181 ≈ 3.0. Step 5: Return 3.0, which is log₂(8).

Step 1: Input a=10, b=100, c=e. Step 2: Compute ln(100) ≈ 4.605170186. Step 3: Compute ln(10) ≈ 2.302585093. Step 4: Compute quotient: 4.605170186 / 2.302585093 ≈ 2.0. Step 5: Return 2.0, which is log₁₀(100).

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Compute Natural Logarithm of a Number
ladders.desc: Calculate the natural logarithm (ln) of a positive real number using standard math functions.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation is used as a building block for computing logarithms in any base via the change-of-base formula. The natural logarithm, ln(x), is the logarithm to the base e (where e ≈ 2.71828). The intuition is that ln(x) gives the time needed to grow to x at a continuous rate, or more abstractly, it's the inverse of the exponential function e^y = x. The algorithm takes a positive number x, uses a math library function (e.g., math.log in Python) to compute ln(x), and returns the result. Time and space complexity are O(1) assuming the math function is constant-time. This connects to L3 by providing log_c values for the change-of-base formula. Common mistakes include passing non-positive values (ln is undefined for x ≤ 0), misunderstanding the base (e.g., using log10 instead), and precision issues for very large or small x. Real code pattern: import math; def natural_log(x): return math.log(x).

examples.trace: Step 1: Input x = 1. Step 2: Compute ln(1) = 0 because e^0 = 1. Step 3: Return 0.0.
Step 1: Input x = e ≈ 2.71828. Step 2: Compute ln(e) = 1 because e^1 = e. Step 3: Return 1.0.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Powers and Exponents
ladders.desc: Grasp the basic idea that a power of two means 2 raised to an integer exponent.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept, essential for beginners to understand what it means for a number to be a power of two. The intuition is that multiplication can be expressed as exponentiation: for example, 2 × 2 × 2 = 2^3 = 8. A power of two is any number that can be written as 2^k, where k is a non-negative integer (including zero, since 2^0 = 1). The sequence starts: 1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32, ... The algorithm to check this conceptually is: given a number n, see if you can repeatedly divide by 2 until you reach 1 without remainder, but here we focus on the definition. Time and space are not applicable as this is conceptual. This feeds into L4 by establishing why we use logarithms (to find k). Common mistakes include thinking 0 is a power of two (it's not), confusing with multiples of 2, or missing that 1 is included. Real code pattern: Conceptual understanding; no code needed, but example: 2^0=1, 2^1=2, 2^2=4, etc.

examples.trace: Step 1: Consider the number 8. Step 2: Check if it can be expressed as 2^k. Step 3: Try k=0: 2^0=1 (no). k=1: 2^1=2 (no). k=2: 2^2=4 (no). k=3: 2^3=8 (yes). Step 4: Conclude 8 is a power of two.

examples.output: No, because no integer k gives 2^k = 6
examples.trace: Step 1: Consider the number 6. Step 2: Check if it can be expressed as 2^k. Step 3: Try k=0: 2^0=1 (no). k=1: 2^1=2 (no). k=2: 2^2=4 (no). k=3: 2^3=8 (no). Step 4: Since no k works, 6 is not a power of two.
